title: Talking About Movies
summary: Alex and Chris chat about movies they have seen recently, share their opinions about different genres, and make plans to see a new film together at the cinema.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Hey Chris, have you seen anything good at the cinema lately?

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: Actually, yes! I watched that new science-fiction film last Saturday. It was incredible.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Oh, the one about the astronauts? I've heard so many good things about it.

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: Yes, that one. The special effects were amazing and the story kept me on the edge of my seat.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: I love films like that. What kind of movies do you usually enjoy?

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: I'm a big fan of action and science fiction. I also enjoy thrillers — films that are exciting and a little scary.

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: I like thrillers too. But my favourite genre is probably romantic comedies. I know some people think they're predictable, but I find them really enjoyable.

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: Ha, fair enough! Have you watched anything good recently?

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: I rewatched an old film last night — a classic romantic comedy from the nineties. It was just as funny as I remembered.

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: That's the best thing about great films — they never get old. Hey, there's a new spy thriller coming out this Friday. Want to go and see it?

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: A spy thriller sounds exciting! What time does it start?

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: There's a seven-thirty showing and a nine o'clock one. Which works better for you?

body.speaker: Alex
body.text: Seven-thirty is perfect. Shall we get something to eat before the film?

body.speaker: Chris
body.text: Great idea. Let's meet at the Italian restaurant near the cinema at six. See you Friday!

annotations.meaningVi: bạn có xem được thứ gì hay không
annotations.exampleEn: Have you read anything good recently?

annotations.phrase: kept me on the edge of my seat
annotations.meaningVi: khiến tôi hồi hộp, căng thẳng từ đầu đến cuối
annotations.pronunciation: /kept miː ɒn ðɪ edʒ əv maɪ siːt/
annotations.exampleEn: The final match kept us on the edge of our seats.

annotations.meaningVi: Tôi rất thích / Tôi là người hâm mộ lớn của
annotations.exampleEn: I'm a big fan of Italian food.
What's your favourite movie genre — action or comedy?
She loves watching romantic comedies on rainy evenings.

annotations.meaningVi: dễ đoán, có thể đoán trước kết quả
annotations.exampleEn: The ending of the film was quite predictable.
I rewatched my favourite film for the third time.

annotations.meaningVi: cổ điển, được coi là kiệt tác không bao giờ cũ
annotations.exampleEn: That song is a true classic — everyone loves it.

annotations.meaningVi: chúng không bao giờ trở nên nhàm chán
annotations.exampleEn: That comedy sketch never gets old — I laugh every time.

annotations.meaningVi: phim hành động gián điệp hồi hộp
annotations.exampleEn: He loves spy thrillers set during the Cold War.
There are three showings of the film on Saturday.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for recent experiences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has + V3) dùng để nói về những trải nghiệm gần đây mà không xác định thời gian cụ thể. Ví dụ: 'Have you seen anything good lately?' / 'I've heard so many good things about it.' So sánh với quá khứ đơn khi thời gian xác định: 'I watched it last Saturday.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use Present Perfect (have/has + past participle) for recent experiences when the exact time is not important. Examples: 'Have you seen anything good lately?', 'I've heard good things about it.' Compare with Past Simple when a specific time is given: 'I watched it last Saturday.'

grammarNotes.title: 'Shall we' and 'Want to' for making suggestions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Shall we + verb?' là cách lịch sự để đề xuất một hoạt động cùng nhau, phổ biến hơn trong tiếng Anh Anh. 'Want to + verb?' hoặc 'Do you want to?' là cách hỏi thân mật hơn. Cả hai đều dùng để rủ ai đó làm điều gì. Ví dụ: 'Shall we get something to eat?' / 'Want to go and see it?'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'Shall we + verb?' to make polite suggestions about doing something together (common in British English). 'Want to + verb?' is a more casual invitation. Both are used to invite someone to join an activity. Examples: 'Shall we meet at six?', 'Want to come to the cinema?'

translationVi:
Alex: Này Chris, dạo này bạn có xem phim gì hay ở rạp không?
Chris: Thực ra là có! Tôi đã xem bộ phim khoa học viễn tưởng mới hôm thứ Bảy tuần trước. Thật tuyệt vời.
Alex: Ồ, phim về các phi hành gia đó à? Tôi đã nghe rất nhiều điều hay về nó.
Chris: Đúng, phim đó. Hiệu ứng hình ảnh thật tuyệt và câu chuyện khiến tôi hồi hộp từ đầu đến cuối.
Alex: Tôi thích những bộ phim như vậy. Thông thường bạn thích xem loại phim nào?
Chris: Tôi là người rất thích phim hành động và khoa học viễn tưởng. Tôi cũng thích phim kinh dị — những bộ phim kịch tính và hơi đáng sợ.
Alex: Tôi cũng thích phim kinh dị. Nhưng thể loại yêu thích của tôi có lẽ là phim hài lãng mạn. Tôi biết một số người nghĩ chúng dễ đoán, nhưng tôi thấy chúng thực sự thú vị.
Chris: Haha, có lý đấy! Gần đây bạn có xem gì hay không?
Alex: Tôi xem lại một bộ phim cũ tối qua — một bộ phim hài lãng mạn cổ điển từ những năm chín mươi. Nó buồn cười đúng như tôi nhớ.
Chris: Đó là điều tuyệt nhất về những bộ phim hay — chúng không bao giờ nhàm chán. Này, có một bộ phim gián điệp hồi hộp mới ra mắt thứ Sáu này. Bạn có muốn đi xem không?
Alex: Phim gián điệp hồi hộp nghe có vẻ thú vị! Phim bắt đầu lúc mấy giờ?
Chris: Có suất bảy giờ ba mươi và một suất chín giờ. Suất nào thuận tiện hơn cho bạn?
Alex: Bảy giờ ba mươi là hoàn hảo. Chúng ta có ăn gì trước khi xem phim không?
Chris: Ý hay đấy. Gặp nhau ở nhà hàng Ý gần rạp chiếu phim lúc sáu giờ nhé. Hẹn gặp bạn thứ Sáu!

questions.prompt: What film did Chris watch last Saturday?
questions.explanation: Chris says 'I watched that new science-fiction film last Saturday.'
questions.hint: Chris mentions the genre and when they saw it.

questions.prompt: What did Chris say about the special effects in the film?
questions.explanation: Chris says 'The special effects were amazing.'
questions.hint: Chris uses a strong positive adjective.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase 'kept me on the edge of my seat' mean?
options: The film was very exciting and tense
questions.explanation: The idiom means the film was very exciting and tense throughout.
questions.hint: It is an idiom describing how a film makes you feel.

questions.prompt: What two genres does Chris say they are a fan of?
questions.explanation: Chris says 'I'm a big fan of action and science fiction.'
questions.hint: Two genres are stated in one sentence.

questions.explanation: Alex says 'my favourite genre is probably romantic comedies.'
questions.hint: Alex says their favourite genre and explains why some people criticise it.

questions.prompt: What criticism do some people have of romantic comedies, according to Alex?
options: They are too expensive to make
questions.explanation: Alex says 'some people think they're predictable.'
questions.hint: A specific criticism is mentioned before Alex defends the genre.

questions.prompt: What did Alex do last night?
questions.explanation: Alex says 'I rewatched an old film last night — a classic romantic comedy from the nineties.'
questions.hint: Alex did not see something new — they watched something again.

questions.prompt: When is the new spy thriller coming out?
questions.explanation: Chris says 'there's a new spy thriller coming out this Friday.'
questions.hint: The day of the week is stated clearly.

questions.prompt: What two showing times are available for the new film?
questions.explanation: Chris says 'There's a seven-thirty showing and a nine o'clock one.'
questions.hint: Both times are mentioned in a single turn.

questions.prompt: Which showing time does Alex choose?
questions.hint: Alex picks one of the two options offered by Chris.

questions.prompt: Where do they agree to meet before the film?
options: At a café near the station
At the Italian restaurant near the cinema
questions.explanation: Chris says 'Let's meet at the Italian restaurant near the cinema at six.'
questions.hint: The meeting place is a specific type of restaurant close to the cinema.

cloze.template:
Alex: Hey Chris, have you seen anything good at the cinema {{b1}}?
Chris: Actually, yes! I watched that new science-fiction film last Saturday. It was incredible.
Alex: Oh, the one about the astronauts? I've {{b2}} so many good things about it.
Chris: Yes, that one. The special effects were amazing and the story kept me on the {{b3}} of my seat.
Alex: I love films like that. What kind of movies do you usually enjoy?
Chris: I'm a big {{b4}} of action and science fiction. I also enjoy thrillers — films that are exciting and a little scary.
Alex: I like thrillers too. But my favourite {{b5}} is probably romantic comedies. I know some people think they're {{b6}}, but I find them really enjoyable.
Chris: Ha, fair enough! Have you watched anything good recently?
Alex: I {{b7}} an old film last night — a classic romantic comedy from the nineties. It was just as funny as I remembered.
Chris: That's the best thing about great films — they never get {{b8}}. Hey, there's a new spy thriller coming out this Friday. Want to go and see it?
Alex: A spy thriller sounds exciting! What time does it {{b9}}?
Chris: There's a seven-thirty showing and a nine o'clock one. Which works better for you?
Alex: Seven-thirty is perfect. Shall we get something to eat before the film?
Chris: Great idea. Let's {{b10}} at the Italian restaurant near the cinema at six. See you Friday!

blanks.explanation: 'lately' — lately means recently; 'Have you seen anything good lately?' asks about recent experiences.
'heard' — you hear things about a film through reviews, friends, or media.
'edge' — 'on the edge of my seat' is an idiom meaning extremely excited or tense.
'fan' — 'a big fan of' means someone who really loves and follows something.
'genre' — a genre is a category of art defined by shared elements, like comedy or thriller.
'predictable' — predictable means you can guess what will happen before it does.
'rewatched' — to rewatch means to watch something again for a second or later time.
'old' — 'never get old' is an idiom meaning something stays enjoyable no matter how many times you experience it.
'start' — Alex asks what time the film starts.
'meet' — Chris suggests they meet at the Italian restaurant before the film.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Alex and Chris enjoy different types of films — science fiction, thrillers, and romantic comedies. Do you think the films and TV shows people enjoy tell us something about their personality or values? Is it possible to enjoy genres that seem very different from each other? Why or why not?
